figures.caption: Figure 7 Normalized density profiles at x = x− (dashed line) and x = x+ (solid line) for the same two simulations shown in Figure 5. The height yp at x = x+ is marked with an asterisk. The vertical dotted line represents the value of ρ̂ = 0.5.

Figure 4 Width- and time-averaged density represented by the salt water volume fraction field ⟨⟨αs⟩⟩ (colours) and streamlines (grey lines) obtained from the width- and time-averaged velocity field. The simulations are the same as in Figure 3, but the extent of the x-axis is much smaller. The horizontal solid grey line at y/H ≈ 1 marks the location of the free surface.
Figure 6 Entrainment coefficient β as a function of q̂air for all simulations. The error bars denote 95% confidence interval

Figure 1 Sketch of the initial lock-exchange configuration (a), with saltwater coloured in dark blue and freshwater coloured with light blue. The two regimes classified by Bacot et al. (2022) are shown in b) the breakthrough regime and c) the curtain-driven regime. The horizontal arrows depict the flow of (secondary) gravity currents and the curved arrows depict the recirculation cells formed in the curtain-driven regime.

Figure 11 Profiles of the width- and time-averaged velocity component ⟨⟨vx⟩⟩ at x = x− (dashed line) and x = x+ (solid line) for the same two simulations shown in Figure 5. The vertical dotted line represents ⟨⟨vx⟩⟩ = 0 and the uppermost location where ⟨⟨vx⟩⟩ = 0 for each profile is marked with an asterisk.

Figure 5 Two examples of the definition of the limits of the bubble curtain and the control volume used for the analysis. The colour denotes the width- and time-averaged air volume fraction ⟨⟨αg⟩⟩. The solid white curves denote the limit of the bubble curtain, and the white dashed lines denote the limits x = x− and x = x+ of the control volume for analysis given by Equations (14) and (15), respectively.

Figure 2 Schematic of a laboratory scale lock with a bubble curtain placed at the centre. The annotations L, W , and Hd depict the length, width and height of the tank, respectively. The mean free-surface level is located at y = H.

Figure 12 Height y0 at which the width- and time-averaged velocity ⟨⟨vx⟩⟩ changes sign for each side of the control volume on a) freshwater side (x = x−) and b) saltwater side (x = x+). The dashed lines are a visual guide marking the sharp increase in y0 when the recirculation cell emerges on the freshwater side.
Figure 10 Peel height yp as a function of Frair for all simulations.

Figure 3 Width-averaged density contours represented by the volume fraction of salt water ⟨αs⟩ for six simulations with different values of Frair and σ at t = tend. The top row shows two cases of the total breakthrough regime, the middle row shows two cases of the diluted breakthrough regime, and the lower row shows two cases of the curtain-driven onset regime. The grey shaded contour depicts the location of the bubble curtain using the region with 0.5 > ⟨αg⟩ > 5× 10−4 and the horizontal solid grey line at y/H ≈ 1 marks the location of the free surface.
Figure 13 Effectiveness as a function of Frair for all simulations.
Figure 8 Normalized density at the lower corners of the control volume as a function of Frair for all simulations on a) the freshwater side (x−, y−) and b) the saltwater side (x+, y−).
Figure 9 Normalized density at the upper corners of the control volume as a function of Frair for all simulations on a) the freshwater side (x−, y+) and b) the saltwater side (x+, y+).
